_meta.description: UNFPA OTG Field App Drug Formulary — comprehensive reproductive health essential medicines
_meta.note: Expanded formulary includes WHO EML essential medicines and UNFPA reproductive health protocols. All entries must be verified by a qualified clinician and approved by the Clinical Advisory Panel before clinical deployment. Source citations reference exact page numbers.

categories.supportive: Antibiotics, pain relief, and supportive care

sources: WHO Model List of Essential Medicines 23rd edition (2023)
WHO PCPNC — Pregnancy, Childbirth, Postpartum and Newborn Care: A guide for essential practice (3rd ed, 2023)
WHO IMPAC/MCPC — Managing Complications in Pregnancy and Childbirth (2nd ed, 2017)
WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use (MEC) 5th edition (2015)
WHO Pocket Handbook for Maternal Mortality Review (2011)
UNFPA Technical Guidance on Contraceptive Methods (2023)

localNames.my: အောက်စီတိုစင်
localNames.km: អុកស៊ីតូស៊ីន

drugs.indication: Active management of third stage of labour (AMTSL); prevention and treatment of postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)
drugs.timing: Within 1 minute of delivery of the baby, before delivery of the placenta
drugs.alternativeDose: For PPH treatment: 20–40 IU in 1 litre IV fluids at 40 drops/min; or 10 IU IM if IV access not available
contraindications: Situations where vaginal delivery is inadvisable
warnings: Do not give as rapid IV bolus — may cause hypotension and cardiac arrhythmia
Has antidiuretic effect — risk of water intoxication with prolonged use in large volumes
Store at 2–8°C; loses potency above 25°C — in hot climates, prefer misoprostol if cold chain unreliable
drugs.source: WHO PCPNC 3rd ed. 2023, Section C3 (Preventing postpartum haemorrhage), Page 89

localNames.km: មីស្សូប្រូស្តូល

drugs.indication: Prevention of PPH where oxytocin unavailable or cold chain unreliable; cervical ripening; early pregnancy failure; incomplete abortion
drugs.timing: Immediately after delivery of the baby
drugs.alternativeDose: PPH treatment (when oxytocin unavailable): 800 mcg sublingually. Cervical priming: 25 mcg vaginally q6h (hospital setting only).
contraindications: Previous uterine scar with >1 prior caesarean (for cervical ripening doses)
warnings: Side effects: fever, shivering, nausea, diarrhoea — inform patient before use
Higher doses (800 mcg SL) for PPH treatment have more side effects — clinical supervision required
Do not use sublingual doses for PPH prevention in facility settings when oxytocin is available
Legal context varies by country — verify local regulations before use for abortion indications
drugs.source: WHO PCPNC 3rd ed. 2023, Section C3; WHO IMPAC/MCPC 2nd ed. 2017

localNames.my: မဂ္ဂနီဆီယမ် ဆာလဖိတ်
localNames.km: ម៉ាញ៉េស្យូម ស៊ុលហ្វាត

drugs.indication: Prevention and treatment of eclamptic seizures; severe pre-eclampsia
drugs.dose: Loading: 4 g IV over 5–10 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV maintenance
drugs.route: IV (or IM if IV access unavailable — see alternative)
drugs.timing: Start immediately on diagnosis of severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia
drugs.alternativeDose: IM loading (if no IV): 5 g IM each buttock (= 10 g total) + 2.5 g IM q4h. Continue for 24 hours after last seizure or delivery.
contraindications: Urine output < 25 mL/hour in preceding 4 hours
warnings: Have calcium gluconate 1 g IV ready as antidote for magnesium toxicity
Monitor: respiratory rate, patellar reflexes, and urine output every 30 minutes
Signs of toxicity: absent patellar reflex, respiratory rate < 12/min — STOP infusion immediately
Antidote: calcium gluconate 1 g IV (10 mL of 10% solution) over 10 minutes

localNames.my: အာမောဇဆလင်
localNames.km: អាម៉ុកស៊ីស៊ីលីន

drugs.indication: Group B streptococcal prophylaxis in labour; treatment of urinary tract infection in pregnancy; postpartum infection (in combination)
drugs.timing: Every 8 hours; duration depends on indication
drugs.alternativeDose: IV: 500 mg–1 g q6–8h for severe infection. For GBS prophylaxis in labour: 2 g IV initial dose, then 1 g IV q4h until delivery.
contraindications: Known penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis) — use erythromycin or clindamycin
warnings: Check for penicillin allergy history before prescribing
For women with penicillin allergy: use local antibiogram-guided alternative
drugs.source: WHO PCPNC 3rd ed. 2023; WHO EML 23rd edition (2023)

localNames.my: သံဓာတ်တံဆိပ်
localNames.km: ជាតិដែក និងអាស៊ីតហ្វូលិក

drugs.indication: Prevention and treatment of iron-deficiency anaemia in pregnancy and postpartum
drugs.dose: 60 mg elemental iron + 400 mcg folic acid
drugs.timing: Once daily throughout pregnancy and 3 months postpartum (prevention). Twice daily for treatment of anaemia.
contraindications: Anaemia not due to iron deficiency (investigate cause)
warnings: Take on empty stomach for best absorption; may cause nausea — take with food if not tolerated
Stools will turn dark — inform the patient this is expected and harmless
For severe anaemia (Hb < 7 g/dL in pregnancy): refer for further assessment
drugs.source: WHO ANC Recommendations 2016, Recommendation A2 (Iron and folic acid supplementation)

localNames.my: လီভိုနော်ဂျက်စတရယ်
localNames.km: លីវូណូហ្គេស្ត្រល

drugs.indication: Emergency contraception (Plan B); regular oral contraceptive (mini-pill)
drugs.dose: 1.5 mg (emergency, single dose); 30 mcg daily (regular pill)
drugs.timing: Within 72 hours of unprotected intercourse (emergency); daily at same time (regular)
drugs.alternativeDose: Can be taken as 2 doses of 0.75 mg within 12 hours, though single dose preferred
contraindications: Current or history of breast cancer (relative — risk-benefit assessment needed)
warnings: Emergency use: NOT a regular contraceptive — counsel on ongoing family planning
Regular use: no protection against STIs — counsel on dual method use
Ectopic pregnancy risk is similar to baseline; high suspicion warranted if pregnant
drugs.source: UNFPA Technical Guidance on Contraceptive Methods 2023; WHO MEC 5th ed. 2015

localNames.my: မိုင်ဖပ်ရီစ်တုံး
localNames.km: មីសេផ្រីស្តូន

drugs.indication: Medical abortion (in pregnancy <13 weeks); preparation for surgical abortion; molar pregnancy; ectopic pregnancy monitoring
drugs.alternativeDose: Low-dose: 200 mg oral, then misoprostol 400 mcg PV/SL at 24–48 hours (WHO recommended)
contraindications: Known or suspected ectopic pregnancy (until ruled out)
Uncontrolled asthma; bleeding disorder or anticoagulant use
warnings: Requires informed consent and counselling on medical abortion process
Heavy bleeding and passage of products — counsel on when to seek help
Follow-up ultrasound at 3–5 days to confirm complete abortion
Legal status varies widely by country — verify local regulations and ethical oversight before use
RhD status must be assessed; RhD-negative women need anti-D immunoglobulin
drugs.source: WHO Abortion Care Guideline (2022); UNFPA guidance on reproductive rights

localNames.my: စက်ထရီယက်စုံ
localNames.km: សេហ្វត្រីយាក់សូន

drugs.indication: Gonorrhoea treatment (first-line); pelvic inflammatory disease; postpartum infection; neonatal ophthalmia prophylaxis
drugs.dose: 250 mg IM (single dose for gonorrhoea); 1–2 g IV/IM q12h for systemic infection
drugs.alternativeDose: For PID: 1 g IV/IM q12–24h + doxycycline. Neonatal eye prophylaxis: 50 mg/mL solution, 1 drop in each eye once.
contraindications: History of severe penicillin allergy (assess cross-reactivity)
warnings: Combination therapy for PID (add doxycycline or azithromycin)
For neonates: preferred agent for ophthalmia prophylaxis and treatment of gonococcal infection
Resistance emerging in some regions — monitor local antibiograms
drugs.source: WHO STI Treatment Guidelines 2022; WHO ANC Recommendations 2016

localNames.my: အဇီသြိုမိုင်စင်
localNames.km: អាស៊ីស្ទ្រូមីស៊ីន

drugs.indication: Chlamydial infection; part of PID regimen; azithromycin-resistant gonorrhoea; macrolide alternative for penicillin allergy
drugs.dose: 1 g oral (single dose for chlamydia); 500 mg daily for 3 days (alternative)
drugs.timing: Single 1 g dose preferred for chlamydia in pregnancy and postpartum
contraindications: Concurrent use with certain cardiac drugs (QT prolongation risk)
warnings: Safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding
Gastrointestinal side effects common — counsel patient
For gonorrhoea: use in combination with ceftriaxone, not monotherapy
drugs.source: WHO STI Treatment Guidelines 2022; WHO PCPNC 3rd ed. 2023

localNames.my: ဒောက်ဆီကလိုင်း
localNames.km: ដុកស៊ីស៊ីលីន

drugs.indication: Chlamydial infection (non-pregnant women); part of PID regimen; non-gonococcal urethritis; syphilis alternative
drugs.dose: 100 mg twice daily for 7 days (chlamydia); higher doses for PID
contraindications: Pregnancy and breastfeeding (risk of permanent tooth staining in fetus/infant)
warnings: CONTRAINDICATED IN PREGNANCY — ensure negative pregnancy test or use reliable contraception
Photosensitivity — counsel on sun protection
GI upset — take with food, but not with milk/antacids (reduced absorption)

localNames.my: ဘင်ဇာတင်းပီနီစီလင်း
localNames.km: ប៉េនីស៊ីលីនជីបេនហាទីន

drugs.indication: Early syphilis (primary, secondary, early latent); congenital syphilis prevention (maternal treatment); yaws
drugs.dose: 2.4 million units IM (single dose for early syphilis); adjusted for weight in neonates
drugs.timing: Single dose; can be repeated weekly × 3 for late latent syphilis
drugs.alternativeDose: Late latent syphilis: 2.4 MU weekly × 3 weeks. Congenital syphilis (neonatal): 50,000 units/kg IV q12h × 10 days
contraindications: Anaphylaxis to penicillins (assess with caution in pregnancy due to serious complications of untreated syphilis)
warnings: Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction common in early syphilis — counsel patient on expected fever, rash, arthralgias lasting 24 hours
Safe in pregnancy for maternal and congenital syphilis
Partner notification and treatment essential
Monitor for penicillin allergy — desensitization may be needed in pregnant women
drugs.source: WHO STI Treatment Guidelines 2022; WHO ANC Recommendations 2016

drugs.indication: Prevention of vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB) in neonates
drugs.dose: 1 mg IM (newborns); 2 mg IM (infants >8 weeks with breastfeeding risk factor)
drugs.route: IM (preferred); oral if IM refused (requires follow-up doses at 2–4 weeks and 6–8 weeks)
drugs.alternativeDose: Oral: 2 mg at birth, 2–4 mg at 3–5 days, 2 mg at 4 weeks (for breastfed infants)
warnings: IM route preferred (single dose, high compliance)
Oral route requires 3 doses — counsel caregivers on importance of follow-up
No contraindication in infants with isoimmune haemolysis
drugs.source: WHO PCPNC 3rd ed. 2023, Section F (Newborn and Infant Health); AAP Recommendations

localNames.my: ငွေ-နိုင်ထရိတ်

drugs.indication: Neonatal ophthalmia prophylaxis (gonococcal and chlamydial prevention); conjunctivitis treatment in resource-limited settings
drugs.dose: 1 drop of 1% solution in each eye
drugs.alternativeDose: Ceftriaxone 50 mg/mL ophthalmic drops preferred in well-equipped facilities. Tetracycline 1% or erythromycin 0.5% eye ointment acceptable alternatives.
contraindications: None — universally indicated for all neonates
warnings: Causes mild chemical conjunctivitis lasting 24–48 hours — counsel parents
Effectiveness depends on proper instillation technique
Not a substitute for maternal antenatal screening and treatment
Store away from light; solution should be clear (discard if discolored)
drugs.source: WHO PCPNC 3rd ed. 2023, Section F; WHO Guidelines on Neonatal Infection

localNames.my: အર်ဂော့တ်-ပစ္စည်းများ
localNames.km: អាលកាឡូអ័រ ergot

drugs.indication: Management of third stage of labour (uterine contraction); prevention and treatment of postpartum haemorrhage (adjunct to oxytocin)
drugs.route: IM, IV, or oral (avoid IV rapid bolus due to severe hypertension risk)
drugs.timing: After delivery of baby and before or after delivery of placenta (depends on protocol)
drugs.alternativeDose: Ergotamine tartrate 0.5 mg sublingual; methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM
contraindications: Coronary artery disease; severe peripheral vascular disease
warnings: NEVER give IV as bolus — risk of severe hypertension and cerebral haemorrhage
Contraindicated in twins until second baby is delivered
Third stage management: oxytocin is preferred first-line agent
Ergot alkaloids reserved for when oxytocin unavailable or ineffective
drugs.source: WHO PCPNC 3rd ed. 2023, Section C3; WHO IMPAC/MCPC 2nd ed. 2017

localNames.my: ဒိုင်ကလိုဖီနက်
localNames.km: ឌីក្លូហ្វេណាក់

drugs.indication: Pain relief (moderate); postpartum pain and cramping; dysmenorrhoea
drugs.dose: 50 mg IM/IV (acute pain); 50 mg orally q6–8h (up to 150 mg/day)
contraindications: Active peptic ulcer disease or gastrointestinal bleeding
warnings: First and second trimester use generally safe; AVOID third trimester
NSAID use in breastfeeding: minimal excretion in milk; generally considered safe
Risk of renal impairment in severely dehydrated women or those with underlying renal disease
Gastrointestinal upset — take with food if oral route used
drugs.source: WHO EML 23rd edition (2023); WHO ANC Recommendations 2016

localNames.my: ပါရာစီတမောလ်
localNames.km: ប៉ារ៉ាសេតាម៉ូល

drugs.indication: Pain relief (mild to moderate); fever reduction in pregnancy and postpartum
drugs.dose: 500–1000 mg orally q4–6h (max 4 g/day); 15–20 mg/kg per dose in children
drugs.route: Oral or rectal (rarely IV in hospital settings)
warnings: Safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding across all trimesters
First-line pain relief and antipyretic in pregnancy
Do not exceed 4 g/day due to hepatotoxicity risk
Combination products may contain multiple sources of paracetamol — counsel on reading labels
drugs.source: WHO EML 23rd edition (2023); WHO ANC Recommendations 2016
